verificationNote: Evidence items are constructed guidance for cloud security audits based on CSA CCM v4, NIST CSF 2.0, and NACSA Act 854 obligations. Not endorsed by any standards body -- verify against authoritative sources before use in formal assessments.
description: Evidence items for cloud security audit -- mapped to CCM control domains, requirements, and applicable regulatory obligations. For auditors conducting cloud security assessments and for security teams preparing for audit.

items.name: MFA enforcement configuration and compliance report
items.description: Evidence that MFA is enforced for all human users accessing cloud management consoles and APIs.
items.howToVerify: Export IAM user list and verify MFA device status for each user. Check conditional access policies for MFA enforcement. Attempt console login without MFA to confirm block.
whatGoodLooksLike: 100% MFA enrollment for all human IAM identities
Conditional access or SCP enforcing MFA for all actions
Hardware security keys on root/owner accounts
Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2) for administrator accounts
commonGaps: MFA not enforced via policy -- relies on user voluntary enrollment
Service accounts with console access but no MFA
Root account MFA enabled but using virtual MFA instead of hardware key
Break-glass accounts without MFA

items.name: Least privilege access review records
items.description: Evidence of periodic access reviews demonstrating least privilege enforcement across cloud identities.
items.howToVerify: Review access review records for last 12 months. Sample 10 IAM policies and check for wildcard (*) actions. Verify IAM Access Analyzer or Recommender findings are actioned.
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly access reviews completed with documented actions
No wildcard (*) actions in production IAM policies
Unused permissions identified and removed via Access Analyzer
Custom roles used where built-in roles are too broad
commonGaps: Broad built-in roles (Administrator, Owner) assigned to multiple users
Access reviews conducted but remediation not completed
No tooling for unused permission detection
Wildcard actions in policies with no review

items.name: Service account and non-human identity inventory
items.description: Evidence that all service accounts, managed identities, and API keys are inventoried, reviewed, and managed with appropriate lifecycle controls.
items.howToVerify: Export service account list across all accounts/projects. Verify each has an owner. Check for long-lived access keys and their last usage date. Confirm workload identity federation where applicable.
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete service account inventory with assigned owners
No access keys older than 90 days without documented exception
Workload identity federation replacing static credentials
Unused service accounts disabled or deleted
commonGaps: Orphaned service accounts with no owner
Access keys older than 1 year still active
Static credentials in CI/CD pipelines instead of OIDC federation
Service accounts with admin-level permissions

items.name: Federated identity and SSO configuration evidence
items.description: Evidence that cloud access is federated through a centralised identity provider with SSO, reducing the risk of password-based attacks and ensuring consistent authentication policies.
whatGoodLooksLike: All cloud accounts federated to centralised IdP (Entra ID, Okta, Google Workspace)
SSO enforced for all cloud management consoles
Local IAM user creation disabled or restricted via SCP/policy
Session timeout and re-authentication policies configured
commonGaps: Local IAM users created outside of federation
SSO configured but bypass via direct console login still possible
No session timeout — sessions persist indefinitely
Federation configured for primary account only — child accounts use local auth

items.name: Privileged access management (PAM) and just-in-time elevation records
items.description: Evidence that privileged access to cloud environments uses just-in-time elevation with time-limited sessions, approval workflows, and comprehensive audit trails.
whatGoodLooksLike: Just-in-time privileged access with time-limited role activation
Approval workflow for privileged role requests
All privileged sessions recorded and auditable
Standing admin access eliminated — zero standing privileges
commonGaps: Permanent admin role assignments without time limits
No approval workflow for privileged access
Privileged sessions not recorded or monitored
Break-glass accounts used for routine operations

items.name: Privileged access management (PAM) configuration
items.description: Evidence that privileged access to cloud environments is managed through a PAM solution with just-in-time elevation, session recording, and approval workflows.
items.howToVerify: Review PAM tool configuration for cloud admin roles. Verify just-in-time access requests require approval. Check session recording is enabled for privileged sessions. Confirm time-bound access with automatic revocation.
whatGoodLooksLike: All privileged access requires just-in-time elevation via PAM
Approval workflow with manager or security team sign-off
Session recording enabled for all elevated access
Time-bound access with automatic revocation after defined period
commonGaps: Standing admin privileges without JIT elevation
PAM configured but bypass paths exist through direct console access
Session recording not enabled or recordings not retained
No approval workflow for emergency access

items.name: Access review and recertification records
items.description: Evidence that cloud IAM access is periodically reviewed and recertified by resource owners, with stale or excessive permissions removed.
items.howToVerify: Request access review reports for the last two cycles. Verify that reviewers are appropriate resource owners. Check that flagged accounts were remediated. Confirm review cadence meets policy requirements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly access reviews completed on schedule
Resource owners perform reviews, not just IT administrators
Remediation of flagged access within defined SLA
Automated tooling to identify unused permissions for review
commonGaps: Access reviews not performed or performed annually instead of quarterly
Reviews rubber-stamped without meaningful evaluation
No tracking of remediation actions from reviews
Service accounts excluded from access review scope

items.name: Cross-account and cross-tenant access inventory
items.description: Evidence that all cross-account trust relationships and cross-tenant access grants are documented, justified, and regularly reviewed.
items.howToVerify: Export cross-account IAM roles and trust policies. Review resource-based policies allowing external account access. Verify each trust relationship has documented business justification. Check for overly permissive trust conditions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete inventory of all cross-account trust relationships
Each relationship has documented business justification and owner
Trust policies use external ID conditions to prevent confused deputy
Regular review cycle for cross-account access
commonGaps: Unknown or undocumented cross-account trust relationships
Trust policies without external ID conditions
Overly permissive cross-account roles granting admin access
No regular review of cross-tenant access

items.name: Identity provider (IdP) hardening and configuration
items.description: Evidence that the identity provider used for cloud SSO is hardened, with secure token lifetimes, conditional access policies, and compromise detection.
items.howToVerify: Review IdP configuration for session timeout settings, token lifetimes, and conditional access policies. Verify impossible travel and anomalous sign-in detection is enabled. Check that IdP admin access is restricted and audited.
whatGoodLooksLike: Short token lifetimes aligned with risk (e.g. 1 hour for access tokens)
Conditional access policies enforcing device compliance and location restrictions
Impossible travel and risky sign-in detection enabled
IdP administrator access restricted to dedicated admin accounts
commonGaps: Long-lived refresh tokens without re-authentication requirements
No conditional access policies beyond MFA
Anomalous sign-in detection disabled or alerts not monitored
IdP admin access from regular user accounts

items.name: Encryption at rest configuration evidence
items.description: Evidence that all data at rest is encrypted across storage, databases, and compute volumes.
items.howToVerify: Run CSPM scan for unencrypted resources. Check S3/Storage default encryption settings. Verify EBS/Disk encryption defaults. Confirm KMS CMK usage for sensitive data.
whatGoodLooksLike: Zero unencrypted storage buckets, database instances, or volumes
Customer-managed keys (CMK) used for sensitive and regulated data
Account-level default encryption enabled
CSPM rule continuously monitoring for unencrypted resources
commonGaps: Provider-managed encryption only -- no CMK for sensitive data
Legacy resources created before encryption defaults were enabled
Temporary or test resources without encryption
No automated detection of unencrypted resource creation

items.name: TLS configuration and minimum version enforcement
items.description: Evidence that TLS 1.2+ is enforced on all services and legacy versions are disabled.
items.howToVerify: Check TLS minimum version settings on load balancers, storage accounts, databases, and API endpoints. Scan externally with SSL Labs or similar. Verify S3 bucket policies deny non-HTTPS.
whatGoodLooksLike: TLS 1.2 minimum on all services (TLS 1.3 preferred where supported)
No TLS 1.0 or 1.1 endpoints
Weak cipher suites disabled
HTTPS-only enforced on storage and API endpoints
commonGaps: Legacy applications still on TLS 1.0/1.1
Storage endpoints accessible via HTTP without redirect
Internal services using unencrypted protocols
Certificate expiry monitoring not configured

items.name: Data classification register and DLP policy evidence
items.description: Evidence that data is classified, labelled, and protected by DLP policies appropriate to classification level.
items.howToVerify: Review data classification policy. Check automated discovery scan results (Macie, Purview, Cloud DLP). Verify DLP policies are active and generating findings. Sample test DLP blocking on classified data.
whatGoodLooksLike: Data classification policy with defined levels (public, internal, confidential, restricted)
Automated data discovery scanning all major data stores
DLP policies blocking exfiltration of classified data
Resource tags reflecting classification level
commonGaps: Classification policy exists but not enforced technically
No automated data discovery -- manual classification only
DLP deployed but in monitor-only mode with no blocking
Classification tags missing from resources

items.name: Data sovereignty and cross-border transfer controls
items.description: Evidence that data residency requirements are enforced and cross-border data transfers are controlled through technical and policy mechanisms.
whatGoodLooksLike: Region restriction policies preventing data creation in non-approved regions
Data flow mapping documenting all cross-border data movement
Geo-replication settings verified to comply with residency requirements
Regular compliance checks for data location via CSPM
commonGaps: No region restriction policies — data can be created in any region
Geo-redundant storage replicating data to non-approved regions
No data flow mapping — unknown cross-border transfers
CDN edge caching exposing data in non-approved jurisdictions

items.name: Sensitive data discovery and automated classification scan results
items.description: Evidence that automated scanning identifies sensitive data across cloud storage, databases, and file shares with findings tracked and remediated.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated discovery scanning all major data stores weekly or continuously
Findings categorised by data type (PII, financial, health, credentials)
Remediation tracking for unprotected sensitive data findings
Integration with DLP policies for automated protection
commonGaps: No automated data discovery — relying on manual classification only
Discovery scanning limited to primary storage — databases and file shares excluded
Findings generated but not reviewed or remediated
No integration between discovery and DLP enforcement

items.name: Data residency and sovereignty compliance
items.description: Evidence that cloud data storage locations comply with data residency requirements and sovereignty regulations.
items.howToVerify: Review cloud resource deployment regions against data residency policy. Check for SCPs or organization policies restricting resource creation to approved regions. Verify data replication does not cross restricted boundaries.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization policies restricting resource deployment to approved regions
Data residency requirements mapped to specific workloads and datasets
Automated enforcement preventing resource creation in unapproved regions
Regular audit of resource locations against residency requirements
commonGaps: No technical enforcement of data residency -- relies on process
CDN or caching layers storing data in unapproved regions
Disaster recovery replication crossing sovereignty boundaries
Managed services using global endpoints without regional restriction

items.name: Database encryption and access controls
items.description: Evidence that all cloud databases have encryption at rest enabled, transparent data encryption configured, and access restricted to authorized applications and administrators.
items.howToVerify: List all database instances and verify encryption status. Check that database credentials are stored in secrets managers. Verify network access is restricted to private subnets. Confirm audit logging is enabled on all databases.
whatGoodLooksLike: All database instances encrypted at rest with customer-managed keys
Database credentials rotated automatically via secrets manager
Network access restricted to application subnets via security groups
Database audit logging enabled and forwarded to SIEM
commonGaps: Legacy databases without encryption at rest
Database credentials hardcoded in application configuration
Databases accessible from public subnets
Audit logging not enabled on all database instances

items.name: Object storage access controls and public exposure
items.description: Evidence that cloud object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) has appropriate access controls preventing unauthorized public exposure.
items.howToVerify: Run storage bucket enumeration and check public access settings. Verify account-level public access blocks are enabled. Review bucket policies and ACLs for overly permissive grants. Check for sensitive data in publicly accessible buckets.
whatGoodLooksLike: Account-level public access block enabled for all storage accounts
No buckets with public read or write access
Bucket policies follow least privilege with specific principal ARNs
Automated scanning for sensitive data exposure in storage
commonGaps: Public access blocks not enabled at account level
Legacy buckets with public ACLs for static website hosting
Bucket policies granting access to wildcard principals
No automated detection of sensitive data in public storage

items.name: Sensitive data discovery and inventory
items.description: Evidence that automated tools are used to discover and inventory sensitive data across cloud storage, databases, and data lakes.
items.howToVerify: Review sensitive data discovery tool configuration and scan results. Verify scan scope covers all storage accounts, databases, and data lakes. Check that discovered sensitive data is classified and tagged. Confirm remediation of improperly stored sensitive data.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated sensitive data discovery running on regular schedule
Scan scope covers all cloud storage, databases, and data processing services
Discovered sensitive data automatically classified and tagged
Alerting and remediation workflow for improperly stored sensitive data
commonGaps: No automated sensitive data discovery tool deployed
Discovery scans limited to specific accounts or storage types
Discovered sensitive data not classified or remediated
Data lakes and analytics services excluded from discovery scope

items.name: Network architecture diagram with segmentation evidence
items.description: Evidence that cloud networks are designed with proper segmentation, subnet isolation, and defence-in-depth architecture.
items.howToVerify: Review network architecture diagram. Verify VPC/VNet separation between environments. Check subnet design (public/private/data tiers). Confirm hub-and-spoke or transit gateway topology.
whatGoodLooksLike: Current network diagram dated within 6 months
Separate VPCs/VNets for production, staging, and development
Public, private, and data subnets with appropriate route tables
Centralised ingress/egress through hub network
commonGaps: Flat network with all workloads in single VPC/VNet
No separation between production and non-production
Diagram outdated or non-existent
Direct internet access from database subnets

items.name: Security group and firewall rule audit results
items.description: Evidence that security groups and firewall rules follow least-privilege deny-by-default configuration.
items.howToVerify: Export all security groups/NSGs/firewall rules. Check for 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on management ports. Verify rules have business justification documentation. Confirm quarterly review records.
whatGoodLooksLike: No 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on SSH (22), RDP (3389), or database ports
All rules documented with business justification and expiry date
Quarterly rule review records with stale rule removal
Automated detection of overly permissive rule creation
commonGaps: 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on management ports (SSH, RDP)
Security groups with hundreds of rules and no documentation
No review process -- rules accumulate over time
Allow-all outbound with no egress filtering

items.name: Private endpoint deployment and public access restriction evidence
items.description: Evidence that PaaS services are accessed via private endpoints and public network access is disabled.
items.howToVerify: List all PaaS resources (databases, storage, key vaults). Verify private endpoints exist. Check public network access is disabled. Confirm DNS resolution uses private zones.
whatGoodLooksLike: Private endpoints deployed for all databases, storage, and key management services
Public network access disabled on PaaS resources
Private DNS zones configured for name resolution
VPC endpoint policies restricting accessible resources
commonGaps: PaaS services accessible from public internet
Private endpoints deployed but public access not disabled
DNS resolution still using public endpoints
No VPC endpoint policies restricting service access

items.name: DDoS protection configuration and incident records
items.description: Evidence that DDoS protection is enabled for internet-facing cloud resources with appropriate mitigation policies and incident response integration.
whatGoodLooksLike: DDoS protection enabled on all internet-facing resources (AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection, GCP Cloud Armor)
Custom DDoS response policies for critical applications
DDoS events integrated with SIEM for detection and alerting
Regular DDoS simulation testing conducted
commonGaps: Basic DDoS protection only — no advanced/standard tier
DDoS protection not enabled on all internet-facing resources
No custom mitigation policies — relying on default thresholds
DDoS events not monitored or integrated with incident response

items.name: WAF configuration and rule management evidence
items.description: Evidence that web application firewalls are deployed on internet-facing applications with active rule sets, tuned for false positives, and regularly updated.
whatGoodLooksLike: WAF deployed on all internet-facing web applications and APIs
Managed rule sets active and updated (OWASP Top 10, bot protection)
Custom rules for application-specific attack patterns
WAF logs integrated with SIEM and reviewed regularly
commonGaps: WAF deployed in detection-only mode (not blocking)
Default rule sets without tuning — excessive false positives
WAF on primary application but not all internet-facing services
No regular rule review or update process

items.name: Network segmentation and micro-segmentation controls
items.description: Evidence that cloud networks implement segmentation between workload tiers and micro-segmentation for sensitive workloads.
items.howToVerify: Review VPC and subnet architecture diagrams. Verify that production, staging, and development environments are in separate VPCs or accounts. Check that application tiers (web, app, data) are in separate subnets with restricted inter-tier traffic. Review network policy or security group rules for micro-segmentation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Environment isolation via separate VPCs or accounts
Application tiers segmented into separate subnets
Inter-tier traffic restricted to required ports and protocols
Micro-segmentation for sensitive workloads using network policies
commonGaps: Flat network with all workloads in a single subnet
Development and production in the same VPC
Security groups allow all traffic between tiers
No micro-segmentation for container or serverless workloads

items.name: Egress traffic controls and data exfiltration prevention
items.description: Evidence that cloud network egress traffic is controlled, monitored, and restricted to prevent data exfiltration.
items.howToVerify: Review egress firewall rules and NAT gateway configurations. Check for VPC flow log analysis of outbound traffic patterns. Verify DNS filtering or proxy for outbound internet access. Confirm alerting on anomalous egress traffic volumes.
whatGoodLooksLike: Egress traffic restricted to approved destinations via firewall rules
Outbound internet access routed through inspection proxy
DNS filtering blocking known malicious domains
Anomalous egress traffic volume alerting configured
commonGaps: Unrestricted egress to the internet from all workloads
No monitoring or analysis of outbound traffic patterns
DNS resolution not filtered or logged
No alerting on large data transfers to external destinations

items.name: DNS security configuration
items.description: Evidence that DNS services are secured with DNSSEC, logging, and protection against DNS-based attacks.
items.howToVerify: Check DNSSEC signing on hosted zones. Verify DNS query logging is enabled. Review DNS firewall rules for blocking malicious domains. Confirm private DNS zones are used for internal service discovery.
whatGoodLooksLike: DNSSEC signing enabled on all public hosted zones
DNS query logging enabled and forwarded to SIEM
DNS firewall blocking known malicious and DGA domains
Private DNS zones for internal service resolution
commonGaps: DNSSEC not enabled on public zones
DNS query logging not enabled
No DNS firewall or filtering for malicious domains
Internal services using public DNS resolution

items.name: TLS certificate management and enforcement
items.description: Evidence that TLS certificates are properly managed with automated renewal, minimum protocol versions, and strong cipher suites.
items.howToVerify: Inventory all TLS certificates and check expiration dates. Verify minimum TLS version is 1.2 or higher. Review cipher suite configuration for deprecated algorithms. Confirm automated certificate renewal via managed certificate services.
whatGoodLooksLike: All public endpoints use TLS 1.2 or higher
Automated certificate management via cloud certificate services
No certificates using deprecated ciphers (RC4, 3DES, SHA-1)
Certificate expiration monitoring with alerting
commonGaps: TLS 1.0 or 1.1 still enabled on load balancers
Manual certificate management leading to expiration incidents
Self-signed certificates used for internal services without PKI
Weak cipher suites enabled for backward compatibility

items.name: Cloud audit logging configuration and coverage report
items.description: Evidence that comprehensive audit logging is enabled across all cloud accounts and regions.
items.howToVerify: Verify CloudTrail/Activity Log/Audit Logs enabled in all accounts and regions. Check data event logging for sensitive resources. Confirm logs stored in protected centralised location.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organisation-level audit trail covering all accounts and regions
Data event logging enabled for sensitive storage and databases
Logs stored in dedicated log archive account with immutable storage
Log integrity validation enabled
commonGaps: Audit logging not enabled in all regions
Data event logging disabled -- only management events captured
Logs stored in same account as production workloads
No log integrity validation

items.name: SIEM integration evidence and detection rule coverage
items.description: Evidence that cloud logs are integrated with SIEM and cloud-specific detection rules are deployed.
items.howToVerify: Verify log sources flowing to SIEM. Check cloud-specific detection rules are active. Review sample alert investigations from last 30 days. Confirm alert routing to on-call personnel.
whatGoodLooksLike: All cloud log sources integrated with SIEM
Cloud-specific detection rules tuned and active
Alert investigation records for last 30 days
Severity-based alert routing with defined SLAs
commonGaps: Only audit logs in SIEM -- missing flow logs, DNS, WAF, threat detection
Generic detection rules without cloud-specific tuning
Alerts generated but not reviewed or investigated
No defined alert response SLAs

items.name: Log retention configuration and compliance evidence
items.description: Evidence that log retention periods meet regulatory and organisational requirements.
items.howToVerify: Check retention settings on log storage. Verify immutable storage configuration. Confirm retention meets regulatory requirements (NACSA s26 if applicable). Review capacity monitoring.
whatGoodLooksLike: Minimum 12 months online retention for audit logs
Immutable storage (WORM) for compliance-required logs
Tiered retention with lifecycle management
Capacity monitoring with alerting before exhaustion
commonGaps: Default retention only (90 days or less)
Logs in modifiable storage without integrity controls
No capacity monitoring -- logs silently deleted on overflow
Retention not aligned to regulatory requirements

items.name: Cloud-native threat detection service configuration and findings
items.description: Evidence that cloud-native threat detection services are enabled and findings are reviewed and actioned.
whatGoodLooksLike: Cloud-native threat detection enabled across all accounts (GuardDuty, Defender for Cloud, SCC)
All finding types enabled including container, DNS, S3/storage, and runtime
Findings triaged within defined SLA (critical: 4h, high: 24h)
Automated suppression rules for known benign patterns with documented justification
commonGaps: Threat detection not enabled in all accounts or regions
Findings generated but not triaged or investigated
Alert fatigue from untuned detection — team ignores findings
No automated response for critical severity findings

items.name: Flow log and network traffic analysis configuration
items.description: Evidence that network flow logs are enabled and analysed for anomalous traffic patterns, lateral movement, and data exfiltration.
whatGoodLooksLike: VPC/VNet flow logs enabled for all production subnets
Flow logs stored in centralised log archive with appropriate retention
Network traffic analysis for anomalous patterns (large transfers, unusual ports)
Flow log data integrated with SIEM or dedicated NTA tool
commonGaps: Flow logs not enabled — no network visibility
Flow logs enabled but not analysed or reviewed
Flow logs in same account as production — modifiable by attackers
Only accepted traffic logged — rejected traffic not captured

items.name: Cloud resource change tracking and audit trail
items.description: Evidence that all cloud resource configuration changes are tracked with full audit trail including who, what, when, and from where.
items.howToVerify: Verify cloud configuration recording service is enabled (AWS Config, Azure Activity Log, GCP Asset Inventory). Check that all resource types are in scope. Confirm change history is retained for required period. Test retrieval of change history for sample resources.
whatGoodLooksLike: Configuration recording enabled for all resource types in all regions
Change history retained for minimum 1 year
Changes correlated with IAM principal and source IP
Configuration snapshots taken on regular schedule
commonGaps: Configuration recording not enabled in all regions
Limited resource types in scope -- missing newer service types
Change history retention shorter than compliance requirements
No correlation between changes and approvals

items.name: Application-level security logging
items.description: Evidence that cloud-hosted applications produce security-relevant logs including authentication events, authorization failures, and input validation errors.
items.howToVerify: Review application logging configuration for security event categories. Verify logs include authentication success and failure events. Check that authorization failures and privilege escalation attempts are logged. Confirm log format includes correlation IDs for request tracing.
whatGoodLooksLike: Applications log authentication, authorization, and security events
Structured log format with correlation IDs for tracing
Security logs forwarded to centralized SIEM
Sensitive data masked or excluded from log entries
commonGaps: Applications only log errors without security event context
No standardized log format across applications
Security logs stored locally and not centralized
Sensitive data (PII, credentials) appearing in log entries

items.name: Log tamper protection and integrity verification
items.description: Evidence that cloud audit logs are protected from tampering with integrity validation, immutable storage, and cross-account log archival.
items.howToVerify: Check log file validation and digest verification settings. Verify logs are stored in immutable storage (S3 Object Lock, WORM). Confirm cross-account log archival to a restricted security account. Test that log deletion is prevented by IAM policies.
whatGoodLooksLike: Log file integrity validation enabled with digest verification
Logs archived to immutable storage with retention lock
Cross-account log delivery to security-owned account
IAM policies preventing log deletion by workload account administrators
commonGaps: Log file integrity validation not enabled
Logs stored in mutable storage without object lock
Same account stores logs and workloads -- admin can delete logs
No verification process for log integrity

items.name: Cost and usage anomaly detection
items.description: Evidence that cloud spending and resource usage patterns are monitored for anomalies that may indicate security incidents such as cryptomining or data exfiltration.
items.howToVerify: Review cost anomaly detection configuration and alert thresholds. Check for alerts on unusual resource provisioning patterns. Verify budget alerts are configured for each account or project. Confirm response procedures for cost anomalies.
whatGoodLooksLike: Cost anomaly detection enabled with automatic alerting
Alerts configured for unusual compute, storage, and data transfer spikes
Budget limits set per account with automatic notification
Response procedures documented for cost anomaly investigation
commonGaps: No cost anomaly detection configured
Alerts only on monthly totals without real-time anomaly detection
No correlation between cost anomalies and security investigation
Budget alerts go to finance team only, not security

items.name: Cloud incident response plan and tabletop exercise records
items.description: Evidence that a cloud-specific IR plan exists and has been tested through tabletop exercises.
items.howToVerify: Review cloud IR plan for completeness. Check last tabletop exercise date and results. Verify CSP engagement procedures are documented. Confirm contact lists are current.
whatGoodLooksLike: Cloud-specific IR plan covering common attack scenarios
Tabletop exercise conducted within last 12 months
CSP support engagement procedure documented
Contact lists current and tested
commonGaps: IT IR plan used without cloud-specific procedures
No tabletop exercises conducted
CSP engagement not documented -- team unsure how to engage cloud vendor during incident
Contact lists outdated

items.name: Forensic readiness configuration and evidence collection procedures
items.description: Evidence that cloud environments are pre-configured for forensic investigation.
items.howToVerify: Verify comprehensive logging enabled. Check forensic snapshot procedures. Confirm dedicated forensic environment exists. Review evidence collection automation.
whatGoodLooksLike: All audit and data access logs enabled
Automated disk snapshot procedure documented and tested
Dedicated forensic account/project with isolated environment
Chain of custody procedures for cloud evidence
commonGaps: Insufficient logging for forensic investigation
No procedure for capturing disk snapshots during incident
Forensic analysis performed in production environment
No chain of custody for cloud evidence

items.name: Containment automation and runbook evidence
items.description: Evidence that automated containment procedures exist for common cloud incident scenarios.
items.howToVerify: Review containment runbooks. Test containment automation in non-production. Verify rollback procedures exist. Check containment SLA targets.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated containment for credential compromise, instance compromise, and data exposure
Containment tested in non-production environment
Rollback procedures for each containment action
Sub-15-minute containment SLA for critical incidents
commonGaps: Containment relies entirely on manual steps
Containment procedures not tested
No rollback procedures -- containment may cause outage
No defined containment SLA

items.name: Regulatory notification procedure and timeline evidence
items.description: Evidence that incident notification procedures meet regulatory timelines (NACSA 6-hour, BNM 24-hour) with documented escalation paths and tested communication channels.
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented notification procedures with regulatory-specific timelines
NACSA 6-hour notification template pre-prepared with required fields
BNM 24-hour notification procedure tested within last 12 months
Escalation matrix current with verified contact details
commonGaps: No awareness of NACSA 6-hour notification requirement
Notification procedures documented but never tested
Single point of failure in notification chain (one person)
Notification templates not aligned to regulatory requirements

items.name: Cloud incident response tabletop exercise records
items.description: Evidence that cloud-specific incident response tabletop exercises are conducted regularly with documented findings and improvement actions.
items.howToVerify: Request tabletop exercise reports from the last 12 months. Verify scenarios include cloud-specific attack vectors. Check that findings resulted in documented improvement actions. Confirm participation from cloud engineering, security, and leadership teams.
whatGoodLooksLike: At least two tabletop exercises per year with cloud-specific scenarios
Scenarios cover common cloud attacks: credential compromise, data exposure, supply chain
Findings result in tracked improvement actions with deadlines
Cross-functional participation including cloud ops, security, legal, and communications
commonGaps: No tabletop exercises conducted or exercises are generic (not cloud-specific)
Findings documented but no improvement actions tracked
Exercises limited to security team without cross-functional participation
Same scenario repeated without addressing previous findings

items.name: Cloud forensic tooling and evidence collection capability
items.description: Evidence that tooling and procedures exist for collecting forensic evidence from cloud environments including disk snapshots, memory captures, and log preservation.
items.howToVerify: Review forensic toolkit documentation and automation scripts. Verify ability to capture disk snapshots and memory dumps from cloud instances. Check that forensic evidence is stored in isolated, tamper-evident storage. Test forensic collection procedure on a sample instance.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated forensic collection toolkit deployed and tested
Procedures for disk snapshot, memory capture, and network capture documented
Forensic evidence stored in isolated account with immutable storage
Chain of custody procedures documented for cloud evidence
commonGaps: No forensic collection tooling or procedures for cloud environments
Forensic evidence collected manually without standardized process
Evidence stored in same account as compromised workload
No chain of custody documentation for cloud forensic evidence

items.name: Automated incident containment playbooks
items.description: Evidence that automated containment playbooks exist for common cloud security incidents including compromised credentials, exposed data, and malicious compute.
items.howToVerify: Review automated containment playbook inventory and triggers. Test containment actions for credential compromise scenario. Verify playbooks include isolation, evidence preservation, and notification steps. Confirm playbooks are tested regularly.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated playbooks for top 5 cloud incident scenarios
Containment actions execute within minutes of detection
Playbooks preserve evidence before containment actions
Regular testing and update cycle for playbooks
commonGaps: No automated containment -- all responses are manual
Playbooks exist in documentation but not implemented as automation
Containment actions destroy evidence needed for investigation
Playbooks not tested or updated after environment changes

items.name: Regulatory breach notification compliance
items.description: Evidence that procedures and templates exist for timely regulatory breach notification in compliance with NACSA, PDPA, and other applicable regulations.
items.howToVerify: Review breach notification procedures and templates. Verify notification timelines align with regulatory requirements (e.g. 72-hour PDPA notification). Check that responsible parties are identified and contact information current. Confirm notification was tested in tabletop exercises.
whatGoodLooksLike: Breach notification procedures with clear timelines per regulation
Pre-approved notification templates for common breach scenarios
Designated notification officers with current contact information
Notification procedures tested in tabletop exercises
commonGaps: No documented breach notification procedure
Notification timelines not mapped to specific regulatory requirements
Templates not pre-approved by legal counsel
Notification procedures never tested in exercises

items.name: Container image scanning results and remediation records
items.description: Evidence that container images are scanned before deployment and critical vulnerabilities are remediated.
items.howToVerify: Review image scanning pipeline configuration. Check scan results for production images. Verify critical/high vulnerability remediation SLA. Confirm deployment blocking for non-compliant images.
whatGoodLooksLike: All images scanned in CI/CD before deployment
Zero critical vulnerabilities in production images
Vulnerability remediation SLA tracked and met
Deployment blocked for images failing scan
commonGaps: Images deployed without scanning
Scanning enabled but results not reviewed
Critical vulnerabilities in production with no remediation timeline
Base images not regularly updated

items.name: Kubernetes CIS benchmark scan results
items.description: Evidence that container orchestrator configurations comply with CIS Kubernetes Benchmark.
items.howToVerify: Run kube-bench or equivalent CIS scan. Review findings and remediation status. Check pod security standards enforcement. Verify RBAC configuration.
whatGoodLooksLike: CIS Kubernetes Benchmark scan within last quarter
Level 1 controls fully implemented
Pod Security Standards (restricted profile) enforced in production
RBAC with least-privilege namespace-level roles
commonGaps: No CIS benchmark scanning
Default pod security settings (no restrictions)
Cluster-admin bound to service accounts unnecessarily
API server accessible from public network

items.name: Container runtime security and anomaly detection evidence
items.description: Evidence that container runtime security monitoring detects anomalous behaviour including unexpected process execution, file system changes, and network connections.
whatGoodLooksLike: Runtime security agent deployed on all container hosts/nodes
Baseline profiles established for expected container behaviour
Alerts generated for anomalous process execution and network connections
Runtime alerts integrated with SIEM and incident response workflow
commonGaps: No runtime security monitoring — only pre-deployment scanning
Runtime agent deployed but alerts not reviewed
No baseline profiling — excessive false positives
Container escape attempts not detected

items.name: Container registry access control and image provenance
items.description: Evidence that container registries enforce access controls and image provenance is verified before deployment.
whatGoodLooksLike: Private container registry with role-based access control
Image signing enabled with verification at deployment (Cosign, DCT)
Only approved base images permitted via admission control
Public registry pull restricted to approved images only
commonGaps: Container registry accessible without authentication
No image signing or provenance verification
Direct pull from public registries (Docker Hub) in production
No approved base image catalogue or governance

items.name: Container network policy enforcement
items.description: Evidence that container network policies restrict inter-pod and inter-service communication to explicitly allowed flows.
items.howToVerify: Review Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources or service mesh configuration. Verify default-deny network policies are applied. Check that inter-service communication is restricted to required paths. Test that unauthorized network connections between pods are blocked.
whatGoodLooksLike: Default-deny network policies in all namespaces
Explicit allow policies for each required communication path
Service mesh enforcing mTLS between services
Network policy violations logged and alerted
commonGaps: No network policies applied -- all pods can communicate freely
Default-deny policy applied but overly broad allow rules negate it
Network policies in production but not in staging or development
No monitoring of network policy violations

items.name: Container secret management
items.description: Evidence that container workloads retrieve secrets from external secret managers rather than embedding them in images, environment variables, or ConfigMaps.
items.howToVerify: Check for secrets stored as Kubernetes Secrets vs external secret references. Verify integration with cloud KMS or external secret manager. Scan container images for embedded credentials. Review pod specifications for environment variable secrets.
whatGoodLooksLike: All secrets retrieved from external secret manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
No secrets embedded in container images or Dockerfiles
Secret rotation implemented without pod restart required
Kubernetes Secrets encrypted at rest with KMS
commonGaps: Secrets stored as plain Kubernetes Secrets without external manager
Credentials embedded in container images or environment variables
No secret rotation -- static credentials with long lifetimes
Kubernetes Secrets not encrypted at rest

items.name: Container resource limits and quotas
items.description: Evidence that containers have CPU and memory limits configured to prevent resource exhaustion and that namespace quotas prevent noisy-neighbor issues.
items.howToVerify: Review pod specifications for resource requests and limits. Check namespace resource quotas and limit ranges. Verify that pods without resource limits are rejected by admission controller. Confirm monitoring of resource utilization against limits.
whatGoodLooksLike: All pods have CPU and memory requests and limits defined
Namespace resource quotas prevent unbounded resource consumption
Admission controller rejects pods without resource limits
Resource utilization monitoring with alerting on approaching limits
commonGaps: Pods deployed without resource limits allowing unbounded consumption
No namespace quotas -- single workload can exhaust cluster resources
Resource limits set but not enforced by admission controller
No monitoring of actual resource utilization against limits

items.name: Container image provenance and signing
items.description: Evidence that container images are signed and verified before deployment, with image provenance tracked through the build pipeline.
items.howToVerify: Check image signing configuration in CI/CD pipeline. Verify admission controller enforces image signature verification. Review image provenance attestations. Confirm that unsigned or untrusted images are rejected at deployment.
whatGoodLooksLike: All images signed in CI/CD pipeline using cosign or Notary
Admission controller verifies image signatures before deployment
Image provenance attestations (SLSA) generated during build
Only images from trusted registries allowed in cluster
commonGaps: Images not signed -- no verification of image provenance
Image signing configured but admission controller does not verify
No image provenance attestations generated during build
Untrusted registries allowed as image sources

items.name: Serverless function IAM role audit and configuration evidence
items.description: Evidence that serverless functions use least-privilege IAM roles and sensitive configuration is properly managed.
items.howToVerify: Export all function IAM roles and permissions. Check for overly permissive policies. Verify secrets stored in secret manager (not environment variables). Review VPC configuration for private resource access.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each function has a dedicated IAM role with minimal permissions
No wildcard (*) resource or action permissions
Secrets stored in secret manager with encrypted references
Functions placed in VPC when accessing private resources
commonGaps: Single shared IAM role for all functions
Admin-level permissions on function roles
Secrets hardcoded in environment variables or source code
No VPC placement for functions accessing databases

items.name: API gateway authentication and rate limiting configuration
items.description: Evidence that API gateways are configured with authentication, rate limiting, and input validation.
items.howToVerify: Review API gateway configuration for authentication requirements. Verify rate limiting and throttling policies. Check request validation rules. Confirm WAF integration.
whatGoodLooksLike: Authentication required on all API endpoints (OAuth 2.0/OIDC)
Rate limiting configured per client
Request schema validation enabled
WAF deployed on API gateway
commonGaps: API endpoints without authentication
No rate limiting -- vulnerable to abuse
No request validation -- accepting arbitrary input
No WAF protection on API endpoints

items.name: Serverless function dependency scanning and runtime monitoring
items.description: Evidence that serverless function dependencies are scanned for vulnerabilities and runtime behaviour is monitored for anomalies.
whatGoodLooksLike: Dependency scanning integrated in serverless build pipeline
Known vulnerable packages blocked from deployment
Runtime monitoring for unexpected API calls and data access patterns
Function timeout and memory limits configured to prevent abuse
commonGaps: No dependency scanning for serverless functions
Outdated dependencies with known vulnerabilities in production
No runtime monitoring — only invocation count metrics
No resource limits — functions can consume unlimited resources

items.name: Serverless event source validation and input sanitisation evidence
items.description: Evidence that serverless function event sources are validated and input is sanitised to prevent injection attacks through event payloads.
whatGoodLooksLike: Event source validation ensuring only authorised triggers invoke functions
Input validation and sanitisation applied to all event payloads
Event schema validation configured on API gateways and event buses
Functions tested for injection attacks via event payloads
commonGaps: No event source validation — any authenticated principal can invoke functions
No input sanitisation — event payloads passed directly to processing logic
No schema validation on API gateway or event bus
No security testing of serverless functions

items.name: Serverless function timeout and concurrency limits
items.description: Evidence that serverless functions have appropriate timeout values, concurrency limits, and resource constraints to prevent abuse and cost overruns.
items.howToVerify: Review function configurations for timeout and concurrency settings. Verify reserved concurrency limits prevent resource exhaustion. Check that function memory and execution time align with expected workload. Confirm cost alerting for unexpected function invocations.
whatGoodLooksLike: All functions have timeout values appropriate to their workload
Reserved concurrency limits prevent runaway invocations
Memory allocation right-sized based on profiling data
Cost alerting configured for unusual invocation patterns
commonGaps: Default maximum timeout on all functions regardless of workload
No concurrency limits -- vulnerable to invocation flooding
Over-provisioned memory increasing cost without benefit
No monitoring of function invocation costs or patterns

items.name: API gateway authentication and rate limiting
items.description: Evidence that API gateways enforce authentication, authorization, and rate limiting on all exposed API endpoints.
items.howToVerify: Review API gateway configuration for authentication requirements. Verify rate limiting and throttling policies per endpoint. Check that API keys or OAuth tokens are required for all endpoints. Test that unauthenticated requests are rejected.
whatGoodLooksLike: All API endpoints require authentication (API key, OAuth, or IAM)
Rate limiting configured per client and per endpoint
Request validation enabled for input schema enforcement
API usage monitoring with anomaly detection
commonGaps: API endpoints accessible without authentication
No rate limiting allowing API abuse and cost escalation
Request validation not enabled -- backend processes all input
No monitoring of API usage patterns for abuse detection

items.name: Serverless event source security
items.description: Evidence that event sources triggering serverless functions are authenticated and validated to prevent event injection attacks.
items.howToVerify: Review event source mappings and trigger configurations. Verify that event sources use authenticated delivery (e.g. signed SNS messages). Check that function code validates event source identity. Confirm that direct invocation is restricted to authorized principals.
whatGoodLooksLike: Event sources authenticated with signature verification
Function resource policies restrict invocation to specific services and accounts
Input validation on event payloads before processing
Direct function invocation restricted to authorized IAM principals
commonGaps: Functions accept events from any source without verification
Resource policies allow invocation from wildcard principals
No input validation on event payloads
Direct invocation allowed from any authenticated principal

items.name: Serverless VPC integration and network isolation
items.description: Evidence that serverless functions accessing sensitive resources are deployed within VPCs with appropriate network controls.
items.howToVerify: Review function VPC configuration for functions accessing databases or internal services. Verify that VPC-connected functions use private subnets. Check security group rules for VPC-connected functions. Confirm NAT gateway or VPC endpoint for internet or service access.
whatGoodLooksLike: Functions accessing internal resources deployed in VPC private subnets
Security groups restrict function network access to required endpoints
VPC endpoints used for AWS service access without internet routing
Functions not requiring internal access deployed without VPC for simplicity
commonGaps: Functions access databases over public endpoints instead of VPC
VPC-connected functions in public subnets with internet gateway
Overly permissive security groups on VPC-connected functions
All functions deployed in VPC regardless of need, increasing complexity

items.name: CI/CD pipeline security configuration and credential management evidence
items.description: Evidence that CI/CD pipelines are hardened with proper secret management and authentication.
items.howToVerify: Review pipeline configuration for secret management. Check authentication method (OIDC vs static credentials). Verify branch protection rules. Confirm build artifact signing.
whatGoodLooksLike: OIDC federation for cloud authentication (no static credentials)
Secrets stored in secret manager (not pipeline variables or code)
Branch protection with required reviews on main branch
Build artifacts signed with provenance
commonGaps: Long-lived access keys stored in pipeline secrets
Secrets in pipeline variables without encryption
No branch protection -- direct push to main allowed
No artifact signing or provenance

items.name: IaC scanning results and SBOM generation evidence
items.description: Evidence that infrastructure-as-code is scanned for misconfigurations and SBOMs are generated for deployments.
items.howToVerify: Review IaC scanning pipeline integration. Check scan results and remediation records. Verify SBOM generation for production builds. Confirm SBOM stored in central repository.
whatGoodLooksLike: IaC scanning integrated in CI/CD with blocking on critical findings
Custom scanning rules for organisation-specific policies
SBOM generated for all production builds (SPDX or CycloneDX)
SBOM repository with lifecycle management
commonGaps: No IaC scanning -- misconfigurations reach production
Scanning enabled but findings not reviewed or remediated
No SBOM generation
No process for CVE-to-SBOM correlation when new vulnerabilities disclosed

items.name: Third-party dependency vulnerability management and SBOM correlation
items.description: Evidence that third-party dependencies are inventoried via SBOMs and new vulnerability disclosures are correlated against deployed software.
whatGoodLooksLike: SBOMs generated for all production builds and stored centrally
Automated CVE-to-SBOM correlation when new vulnerabilities disclosed
Vulnerability response SLA tracked for third-party dependencies
Dependency update automation (Dependabot, Renovate) enabled
commonGaps: No SBOM generation — unknown third-party dependencies in production
SBOMs generated but no CVE correlation process
No response SLA for third-party vulnerability remediation
Manual dependency updates — no automation

items.name: Container and IaC supply chain integrity evidence
items.description: Evidence that container base images and IaC modules are sourced from trusted, verified origins with integrity checks.
whatGoodLooksLike: Approved base image catalogue maintained and enforced via admission control
IaC modules sourced from approved private registry with version pinning
Container images and IaC modules scanned for embedded secrets and backdoors
Supply chain provenance tracked using SLSA or equivalent framework
commonGaps: Base images pulled directly from public Docker Hub without verification
IaC modules sourced from public registries without review or pinning
No scanning for embedded secrets in container images
No supply chain provenance tracking

items.name: CI/CD pipeline access controls and audit trail
items.description: Evidence that CI/CD pipeline access is restricted with role-based permissions, approval gates, and complete audit trail of pipeline executions.
items.howToVerify: Review CI/CD platform access controls and role assignments. Verify approval gates for production deployments. Check audit logs for pipeline configuration changes. Confirm separation of duties between pipeline developers and approvers.
whatGoodLooksLike: Role-based access control on pipeline configuration and execution
Mandatory approval gates for production deployments
Complete audit trail of pipeline executions and configuration changes
Separation of duties -- pipeline authors cannot approve their own deployments
commonGaps: All developers have admin access to CI/CD platform
No approval gates -- code merges automatically deploy to production
Pipeline execution logs not retained or audited
Same person can author, approve, and deploy pipeline changes

items.name: Dependency vulnerability scanning and remediation
items.description: Evidence that software dependencies are scanned for vulnerabilities with documented remediation timelines and exception processes.
items.howToVerify: Review dependency scanning tool configuration and integration with CI/CD. Check vulnerability report for open findings and remediation status. Verify that critical and high vulnerabilities block pipeline. Confirm exception process for accepted vulnerabilities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Dependency scanning integrated in CI/CD pipeline blocking on critical/high findings
Vulnerability database updated continuously with new CVEs
Remediation SLAs: critical 24h, high 7d, medium 30d
Exception process requiring security team approval with expiration date
commonGaps: Dependency scanning not integrated in CI/CD -- runs ad hoc
Scanning enabled but does not block pipeline on findings
Large backlog of unaddressed vulnerability findings
No defined remediation timelines or exception process

items.name: Infrastructure as Code security review process
items.description: Evidence that infrastructure as code templates undergo security review with automated scanning and peer review before deployment.
items.howToVerify: Review IaC scanning tool configuration and policy rules. Check that IaC changes require security review in pull request process. Verify scanning catches common misconfigurations. Confirm that high-severity IaC findings block deployment.
whatGoodLooksLike: IaC scanning integrated in pull request workflow with automated comments
Policy rules cover CIS benchmarks and cloud security best practices
High-severity findings block merge and deployment
Security team reviews IaC changes for sensitive resources
commonGaps: IaC scanning not integrated in development workflow
Scanning rules are defaults without customization for organization
Findings are informational only and do not block deployment
No security review for IaC changes to sensitive resources

items.name: Build environment security and integrity
items.description: Evidence that CI/CD build environments are secured with ephemeral runners, minimal tooling, and protection against build-time attacks.
items.howToVerify: Review build runner configuration for ephemeral vs persistent runners. Check that build environments use minimal base images. Verify that build secrets are injected securely and not persisted. Confirm that build outputs are signed or attested.
whatGoodLooksLike: Ephemeral build runners destroyed after each job
Minimal base images for build environments without unnecessary tools
Build secrets injected via secure mechanism and not persisted to disk
Build outputs include provenance attestation (SLSA level 2+)
commonGaps: Persistent build runners reused across jobs and teams
Build environments with broad tooling that could be exploited
Build secrets stored in environment variables accessible to all steps
No build output signing or provenance attestation

items.name: KMS key inventory and access policy evidence
items.description: Evidence that all encryption operations use cloud KMS and key access policies enforce least privilege.
items.howToVerify: Export KMS key inventory. Verify key policies restrict access to authorised principals. Check for any application-managed keys outside KMS. Confirm audit logging enabled for all key operations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete KMS key inventory with purpose and owner for each key
Key policies enforce least-privilege access
No encryption keys stored in application code or config files
All KMS API calls logged in audit trail
commonGaps: Encryption keys stored in application configuration files
KMS key policies with broad access (full account access)
No key usage audit logging
Orphaned keys with no documented purpose or owner

items.name: Key rotation configuration and compliance evidence
items.description: Evidence that automatic key rotation is enabled and rotation schedules are appropriate to data sensitivity.
items.howToVerify: Check automatic rotation settings on all KMS keys. Verify rotation periods align to data classification. Confirm old key versions are disabled after grace period. Review rotation audit logs.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automatic rotation enabled on all KMS keys
Rotation period aligned to data sensitivity (90 days sensitive, 365 days standard)
Old key versions disabled after grace period
Rotation compliance monitored via CSPM
commonGaps: Automatic rotation not enabled
Keys older than 2 years with no rotation
No monitoring of rotation compliance
Old key versions never disabled

items.name: Certificate management and automated renewal evidence
items.description: Evidence that TLS/SSL certificates are inventoried, monitored for expiry, and automatically renewed where possible.
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete certificate inventory with expiry dates and owners
Automated certificate renewal via ACM, Let's Encrypt, or equivalent
Certificate expiry monitoring with alerting at 30 and 7 days
No expired certificates in production environments
commonGaps: No certificate inventory — certificates discovered only on expiry
Manual certificate renewal process — prone to missed renewals
No expiry monitoring or alerting
Self-signed certificates used in production without documented exception

items.name: Hardware security module (HSM) and key isolation evidence
items.description: Evidence that sensitive encryption operations use dedicated HSM or equivalent key isolation for regulatory or high-security requirements.
whatGoodLooksLike: HSM-backed keys (CloudHSM, Dedicated HSM, Cloud HSM) for regulatory-required workloads
Key isolation between tenants verified via CSP attestation
HSM audit logs independently monitored
FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or equivalent compliance verified for HSM
commonGaps: No HSM usage — all keys in software-based KMS
HSM deployed but key isolation not verified via CSP attestation
HSM audit logs not monitored independently
FIPS compliance level not verified or insufficient for regulatory requirements

items.name: Key access policy and usage audit
items.description: Evidence that encryption key access policies follow least privilege and that key usage is audited for unauthorized access attempts.
items.howToVerify: Review KMS key policies for overly broad access grants. Check key usage logs for unauthorized or anomalous access patterns. Verify that key administrators cannot use keys for encryption and vice versa. Confirm separation of duties between key management and key usage roles.
whatGoodLooksLike: Key policies grant access only to specific services and principals
Key usage logged and monitored for anomalous access patterns
Separation of duties: key admins cannot encrypt/decrypt and operators cannot manage keys
Regular review of key access policies
commonGaps: Key policies grant access to root account or all principals in account
Key usage not monitored or audited
Same role can manage keys and use them for encryption
Key policies never reviewed after initial creation

items.name: Cryptographic key lifecycle management
items.description: Evidence that encryption keys follow a defined lifecycle including creation, activation, rotation, deactivation, and destruction with documented procedures for each phase.
items.howToVerify: Review key lifecycle management procedures and documentation. Verify key states are tracked (active, suspended, deactivated, destroyed). Check that key destruction procedures include waiting periods and multi-party authorization. Confirm old key versions are deactivated on schedule.
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented key lifecycle policy covering all phases
Automated key rotation with configurable schedules
Multi-party authorization required for key destruction
Key state tracking with scheduled transitions
commonGaps: No documented key lifecycle procedures
Keys created but never rotated or destroyed
Single administrator can destroy keys without approval
No tracking of key states or scheduled transitions

items.name: Certificate and TLS key management
items.description: Evidence that TLS certificates and their private keys are managed securely with automated issuance, renewal, and revocation capabilities.
items.howToVerify: Review certificate inventory and management tooling. Verify automated renewal via cloud certificate manager or internal PKI. Check certificate revocation procedures and CRL/OCSP configuration. Confirm private keys are stored in HSM or KMS.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated certificate issuance and renewal via managed service
Certificate inventory with expiration monitoring and alerting
Private keys stored in HSM or cloud KMS -- never exported
Certificate revocation procedures tested and documented
commonGaps: Manual certificate management leading to expiration outages
Private keys stored on disk or in version control
No certificate inventory -- unknown certificates in environment
No certificate revocation procedure or CRL distribution

items.name: Data encryption key envelope architecture
items.description: Evidence that envelope encryption is used with data encryption keys (DEKs) protected by key encryption keys (KEKs) in a hierarchical key management architecture.
items.howToVerify: Review encryption architecture documentation for envelope encryption pattern. Verify DEKs are encrypted by KEKs stored in KMS. Check that DEKs are generated per-object or per-resource. Confirm KEK rotation re-wraps DEKs without data re-encryption.
whatGoodLooksLike: Envelope encryption with DEKs protected by KEKs in cloud KMS
Unique DEKs per object or resource for blast radius limitation
KEK rotation re-wraps DEKs without requiring data re-encryption
Encryption architecture documented and reviewed by security team
commonGaps: Single encryption key used for all data without envelope encryption
Key rotation requires full data re-encryption causing operational issues
Encryption architecture not documented or understood by team
DEKs stored alongside encrypted data without protection

items.name: CSPM scan results and remediation records
items.description: Evidence that CSPM tooling is deployed and findings are tracked and remediated within defined SLAs.
items.howToVerify: Review CSPM dashboard for current compliance score. Check finding remediation records against SLA (critical: 24h, high: 7d, medium: 30d). Verify coverage across all accounts.
whatGoodLooksLike: CSPM enabled across all cloud accounts and regions
Compliance score tracked over time with improving trend
Critical and high findings remediated within SLA
Automated remediation for common findings
commonGaps: CSPM not deployed or only in primary account
Findings generated but not tracked or remediated
No defined remediation SLAs
Alert fatigue from untuned CSPM rules

items.name: CIS benchmark compliance report and exception register
items.description: Evidence of CIS benchmark compliance assessment and documented exceptions.
items.howToVerify: Review most recent CIS benchmark scan results. Check compliance percentage by section. Review exception register for non-compliant items with risk acceptance.
whatGoodLooksLike: CIS Level 1 fully compliant in all production accounts
CIS Level 2 applied to production workloads
Exception register with risk acceptance for non-compliant items
Continuous monitoring with automated alerting on new non-compliance
commonGaps: No CIS benchmark assessment conducted
Assessment conducted once but never repeated
Non-compliance with no exception register or risk acceptance
CIS assessment in primary account only -- not all accounts

items.name: Resource tagging compliance report
items.description: Evidence that mandatory resource tags are enforced across all cloud resources.
items.howToVerify: Run tagging compliance report across all accounts. Check enforcement policies (deny creation without tags). Verify mandatory tag set includes owner, environment, data-classification, cost-centre.
whatGoodLooksLike: Greater than 95% tagging compliance across all resources
Policy enforcement denying untagged resource creation
Mandatory tags: owner, environment, data-classification, cost-centre
Tagging compliance tracked and reported monthly
commonGaps: No mandatory tags defined or enforced
Tags defined but no enforcement mechanism
Low compliance percentage with no improvement plan
Tags applied inconsistently with no standard taxonomy

items.name: Drift detection and automated remediation evidence
items.description: Evidence that configuration drift from approved baselines is detected and automatically remediated for critical resources.
whatGoodLooksLike: Configuration drift detection enabled for all production resources
Automated remediation for critical drift (e.g., public storage re-secured)
Drift events logged and tracked with resolution records
Baseline configurations version-controlled as infrastructure-as-code
commonGaps: No drift detection — configuration changes go unnoticed
Drift detected but no automated remediation
Baseline configurations not documented or version-controlled
Manual changes made directly in console without tracking

items.name: Automated remediation for critical misconfigurations
items.description: Evidence that automated remediation is configured for critical cloud misconfigurations such as public access, missing encryption, and overly permissive security groups.
items.howToVerify: Review automated remediation rules and triggers. Verify that critical misconfigurations trigger automatic corrective action. Check remediation logs for execution history. Confirm that remediation does not cause service disruption.
whatGoodLooksLike: Auto-remediation for top 10 critical misconfigurations
Remediation rules tested in non-production before production deployment
Remediation execution logged with notification to resource owners
Guardrails prevent remediation from causing service disruption
commonGaps: No automated remediation -- all corrections are manual
Auto-remediation configured but not tested, causing outages
Remediation limited to alerting without corrective action
Resource owners not notified of automated corrections

items.name: Multi-account security baseline enforcement
items.description: Evidence that a security baseline is enforced across all cloud accounts or projects using organization-level policies (SCPs, Organization Policies, Azure Policies).
items.howToVerify: Review organization-level policy configuration (SCPs, OrgPolicies). Verify baseline includes required security controls (encryption, logging, network). Check that new accounts automatically inherit security baseline. Confirm policy exceptions are documented and approved.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization-level policies enforce security baseline on all accounts
Baseline includes: encryption, logging, network restrictions, region restrictions
New accounts automatically provisioned with security baseline
Policy exceptions require security team approval with documented justification
commonGaps: No organization-level security policies -- each account configured independently
Baseline policies exist but not applied to all organizational units
New accounts provisioned without security baseline
Exceptions granted without formal process or expiration

items.name: Cloud resource inventory and ownership tracking
items.description: Evidence that all cloud resources are inventoried with defined ownership, cost center, and data classification tags.
items.howToVerify: Review resource tagging policy and compliance reports. Check that required tags (owner, cost center, data classification, environment) are enforced. Verify tag compliance percentage across all resources. Confirm untagged resources are identified and remediated.
whatGoodLooksLike: Mandatory tags enforced via tag policies or admission webhooks
Greater than 95% tag compliance across all resources
Automated reporting on untagged or non-compliant resources
Tag values validated against approved lists (cost centers, teams)
commonGaps: No tag policy enforcement -- tagging is voluntary
Low tag compliance making cost allocation and ownership unclear
Tag keys inconsistent across teams (owner vs Owner vs resource-owner)
No automated reporting on tag compliance

items.name: Service enablement and API controls
items.description: Evidence that cloud service APIs are restricted to approved services with unapproved or risky services blocked at the organization level.
items.howToVerify: Review organization policies for service restriction (SCP deny lists, GCP Organization Policy constraints). Verify that only approved services are available in workload accounts. Check exception process for enabling new services. Confirm that high-risk services require additional approval.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization policies restrict available services to approved list
New service enablement requires security review and approval
High-risk services (e.g. public-facing, data processing) require additional controls
Regular review of approved service list against actual usage
commonGaps: All cloud services available without restriction
No security review process for new service adoption
Service restrictions applied inconsistently across accounts
No visibility into which services are actively used

items.name: Backup configuration and coverage audit
items.description: Evidence that all cloud workloads are covered by appropriate backup policies.
items.howToVerify: Review backup configuration for all databases, compute, and storage. Verify backup frequency aligns to RPO. Check backup encryption and immutability settings. Confirm backup coverage against resource inventory.
whatGoodLooksLike: All production databases and compute backed up per policy
Backup frequency aligned to RPO requirements
Backups encrypted with customer-managed keys
Immutable backups (vault lock) for ransomware protection
commonGaps: Backup not configured for all production resources
Backup frequency not aligned to RPO -- potential data loss exceeds tolerance
Backups in same account as production -- vulnerable to account compromise
No immutable backups -- vulnerable to ransomware

items.name: DR test results and RTO/RPO validation
items.description: Evidence that DR testing has been conducted and RTO/RPO targets have been validated.
items.howToVerify: Review last DR test report. Compare actual recovery time to RTO target. Verify RPO was met (no data loss beyond tolerance). Check remediation actions from previous tests.
whatGoodLooksLike: DR test conducted within last 12 months
Actual recovery time within defined RTO
Data loss within defined RPO
Remediation actions from previous tests completed
commonGaps: No DR testing conducted
DR test limited to backup restore -- no full failover test
RTO/RPO targets not defined or not validated
Test findings not remediated

items.name: Cross-region and cross-account backup replication evidence
items.description: Evidence that critical backups are replicated to a separate region and/or account to protect against regional outages and account compromise.
whatGoodLooksLike: Critical backups replicated to a different region from primary
Backup copies stored in a separate cloud account with independent credentials
Cross-region restore tested and validated within last 12 months
Replication lag monitored with alerting on delays
commonGaps: Backups stored in same region as production — single region failure risk
Backups in same account — account compromise deletes production and backups
Cross-region restore never tested
No monitoring of replication lag

items.name: Ransomware protection and immutable backup configuration
items.description: Evidence that backups are protected against ransomware through immutability, air-gapping, or other tamper-resistant mechanisms.
whatGoodLooksLike: Immutable backups (vault lock, object lock, WORM) for all critical data
Backup deletion requires MFA and multi-person approval
Backup integrity verification automated and scheduled
Recovery from ransomware scenario tested in tabletop or simulation
commonGaps: No immutable backups — attacker with admin access can delete all backups
Backup deletion possible with single administrator account
No backup integrity verification
Ransomware recovery scenario never tested

items.name: Backup encryption and access controls
items.description: Evidence that all backups are encrypted at rest with controlled access and that backup encryption keys are managed separately from production keys.
items.howToVerify: Verify all backup vaults and snapshots are encrypted. Check that backup access policies restrict access to backup administrators only. Verify backup encryption keys are separate from production data keys. Confirm backup access is logged and monitored.
whatGoodLooksLike: All backups encrypted with customer-managed keys
Backup vault access restricted to dedicated backup administrator role
Backup encryption keys managed separately from production keys
Backup access logged and monitored for unauthorized access
commonGaps: Backups encrypted with default service keys instead of customer-managed
Backup access available to all administrators in the account
Same encryption keys used for backups and production data
Backup access not logged or monitored

items.name: Backup restoration testing records
items.description: Evidence that backup restoration is tested regularly with documented results including restoration time, data integrity verification, and comparison against RTO/RPO targets.
items.howToVerify: Request backup restoration test reports for the last 12 months. Verify restoration was tested for critical systems. Check that actual restoration time is compared against RTO target. Confirm data integrity verification was performed after restoration.
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly restoration testing for critical systems
Actual restoration times documented and within RTO targets
Data integrity verified after restoration using checksums or application validation
Test findings result in improvement actions for backup procedures
commonGaps: Backup restoration never tested -- assumed to work
Restoration tested but results not documented
Restoration time exceeds RTO target without remediation plan
Data integrity not verified after test restoration

items.name: Ransomware-resilient backup architecture
items.description: Evidence that backup architecture is resilient to ransomware with immutable backups, air-gapped copies, and protection against administrative compromise.
