items.howToVerify: Verify backup immutability settings (vault lock, object lock). Check for air-gapped or cross-account backup copies. Verify that backup deletion requires multi-party authorization. Confirm backups cannot be encrypted or deleted by compromised admin credentials.
whatGoodLooksLike: Immutable backups with vault lock preventing deletion
Cross-account backup copies in isolated security account
Multi-party authorization required for backup deletion or policy changes
Backup retention independent of production account administrators
commonGaps: Backups not immutable -- can be deleted by account administrator
No cross-account or air-gapped backup copies
Single administrator can delete backups without approval
Backup account accessible from production environment

items.name: Disaster recovery failover and failback procedures
items.description: Evidence that disaster recovery failover and failback procedures are documented, automated where possible, and tested with actual failover exercises.
items.howToVerify: Review DR failover and failback runbooks. Check automation for DNS failover, database promotion, and service redirection. Verify DR exercise reports with actual failover execution. Confirm failback procedures are tested in addition to failover.
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented failover and failback runbooks for all critical systems
Automated failover for DNS, load balancers, and database promotion
Annual DR exercise with actual failover to secondary region
Failback procedures tested and documented
commonGaps: DR procedures documented but never tested with actual failover
Manual failover process taking hours instead of minutes
Failover tested but failback procedures not documented or tested
DR exercise limited to tabletop without actual system failover

items.name: Cloud governance framework and landing zone evidence
items.description: Evidence that a cloud governance framework is established with guardrails, policies, and accountability.
items.howToVerify: Review cloud governance policy document. Check account/subscription structure. Verify SCPs/policies enforcing guardrails. Confirm landing zone deployment.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board-approved cloud security policy
Landing zone deployed with security guardrails
Account vending process with security baseline
Approved service catalogue and architecture patterns
commonGaps: No formal cloud governance framework
Accounts created ad hoc without security baseline
No guardrails -- relying on user awareness for security
Governance framework exists but not enforced

items.name: Policy-as-code deployment and compliance dashboard
items.description: Evidence that security policies are implemented as code with automated enforcement.
items.howToVerify: Review policy-as-code repository. Verify policies deployed across all accounts. Check compliance dashboard for policy adherence. Confirm exception management process.
whatGoodLooksLike: Security policies managed as code in version control
Policies deployed via CI/CD to all accounts
Compliance dashboard showing policy adherence percentage
Time-limited exceptions with documented risk acceptance
commonGaps: Policies documented but not implemented as code
Policy-as-code deployed in primary account only
No compliance dashboard or monitoring
Permanent exceptions without review

items.name: CSP compliance reports and vendor assessment records
items.description: Evidence that CSP compliance reports are collected and vendor security assessments conducted.
items.howToVerify: Request current CSP SOC 2 and ISO 27001 reports. Review shared responsibility model documentation. Check SaaS vendor assessment records. Verify CAIQ responses reviewed.
whatGoodLooksLike: Current CSP SOC 2 Type II report reviewed within last 12 months
Shared responsibility model documented with obligations mapped
SaaS vendor assessments completed for critical vendors
CAIQ or equivalent questionnaire responses reviewed
commonGaps: CSP compliance reports never requested or reviewed
Shared responsibility model not understood -- security gaps at boundary
No SaaS vendor security assessment process
Relying solely on CSP certifications without reviewing report content

items.name: Cloud cost governance and anomaly detection evidence
items.description: Evidence that cloud spending is monitored with budgets, alerts, and anomaly detection to identify compromised accounts or resource hijacking.
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly budgets set for all cloud accounts with alert thresholds
Cost anomaly detection enabled with automated alerting
Anomalous spending investigated within 24 hours
Unused resources identified and cleaned up monthly
commonGaps: No cloud budgets or spending alerts configured
Cost anomalies not monitored — cryptomining would go undetected
Anomaly alerts generated but not investigated
Significant unused resource waste with no cleanup process

items.name: Cloud security training and awareness records
items.description: Evidence that staff with cloud responsibilities receive appropriate security training covering cloud-specific risks, shared responsibility, and operational procedures.
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual cloud security training for all cloud administrators
Role-specific training paths (architect, developer, operator, auditor)
Training completion tracked with compliance reporting
Cloud security certifications (CCSK, CCSP) for key staff
commonGaps: No cloud-specific security training — general IT security only
Training available but not tracked or enforced
Training content outdated — does not cover current cloud services
No certification requirements for cloud security roles

items.name: Cloud risk register and treatment plans
items.description: Evidence that a cloud-specific risk register is maintained with identified risks, risk owners, treatment plans, and residual risk acceptance.
items.howToVerify: Review cloud risk register for completeness and currency. Verify each risk has an assigned owner and treatment plan. Check that risk assessments are updated after significant changes. Confirm residual risks are formally accepted by appropriate authority.
whatGoodLooksLike: Cloud-specific risk register maintained and updated quarterly
Each risk has assigned owner, likelihood, impact, and treatment plan
Risk assessments updated after architecture changes or incidents
Residual risk formally accepted by CISO or risk committee
commonGaps: No cloud-specific risk register -- cloud risks not separately tracked
Risk register exists but not updated regularly
Risks identified but no treatment plans or owners assigned
Residual risk not formally accepted by appropriate authority

items.name: Cloud security training and awareness program
items.description: Evidence that cloud security training is provided to development and operations teams with role-specific content and completion tracking.
items.howToVerify: Review cloud security training program curriculum and schedule. Check training completion rates for relevant teams. Verify training content covers cloud-specific threats and controls. Confirm training is updated regularly to reflect new services and threats.
whatGoodLooksLike: Role-specific cloud security training for developers, operators, and architects
Training completion tracked with greater than 90% completion rate
Training content updated annually to reflect new cloud services and threats
Hands-on labs and exercises supplement classroom or online training
commonGaps: No cloud-specific security training -- only generic security awareness
Training not tracked or low completion rates
Training content outdated and not reflecting current cloud services
Training is lecture-only without hands-on exercises

items.name: Cloud shared responsibility model documentation
items.description: Evidence that the shared responsibility model is documented for each CSP, with clear delineation of customer vs provider responsibilities and gap analysis.
items.howToVerify: Review shared responsibility documentation for each CSP in use. Verify that responsibilities are mapped to specific security controls. Check that gaps in the customer responsibility area are identified with mitigation plans. Confirm documentation is reviewed when adopting new cloud services.
whatGoodLooksLike: Shared responsibility matrix documented for each CSP
Customer responsibilities mapped to specific security controls and evidence
Gaps identified with mitigation or risk acceptance plans
Documentation updated when adopting new services or changing architecture
commonGaps: No documented shared responsibility understanding
Assumption that CSP handles security responsibilities that are customer-owned
Shared responsibility understood for IaaS but not for PaaS/SaaS services
Documentation not updated for new services

items.name: Regulatory compliance mapping and gap analysis
items.description: Evidence that applicable regulatory requirements (NACSA, PDPA, etc.) are mapped to cloud security controls with gap analysis and remediation tracking.
items.howToVerify: Review regulatory compliance mapping matrix. Verify each applicable requirement is mapped to implementing control(s). Check gap analysis for unmapped or partially implemented requirements. Confirm remediation tracking for identified gaps.
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete mapping of regulatory requirements to cloud security controls
Gap analysis identifying partially or unimplemented requirements
Remediation plan with timelines for closing identified gaps
Regular review and update of mapping as regulations change
commonGaps: No formal mapping between regulations and cloud controls
Mapping exists but incomplete -- newer regulations not covered
Gaps identified but no remediation plan or timeline
Mapping not updated when regulations are amended

items.name: Cloud service provider audit reports and certifications
items.description: Evidence that CSP audit reports (SOC 2, ISO 27001, etc.) are obtained, reviewed, and any relevant findings tracked for customer impact assessment.
items.howToVerify: Request copies of CSP audit reports (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 certificates). Verify reports cover the services and regions in use. Check that complementary user entity controls (CUECs) are implemented. Confirm any CSP audit findings are assessed for customer impact.
whatGoodLooksLike: Current SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 reports obtained for all CSPs
Reports reviewed for coverage of services and regions in use
CUECs from SOC reports implemented and evidenced
CSP audit findings assessed for customer environment impact
commonGaps: CSP audit reports not obtained or reviewed
Reports obtained but CUECs not implemented
Reports cover different services or regions than those in use
No assessment of CSP findings for customer impact
